It took some time but here are my thoughts.

**Client field**
According to what we discussed the client field is not required because it's difficult to define what should be put in it. For example on iOS you cannot get any unique device ID. That means that anyway the client needs to generate some random sequence in order to fill client field or it should be randomly filled on server side.

We concluded that client field should be removed. However knowing that we client field was invented to protect against overflowing database with new tokens for the same users we decided to introduce expiration date to the token model. We should also provide a logic for removing expired tokens.

Setting like `MAXIMUM_CLIENTS_NUMBER` is also a good idea.

**The app name**
I would opt in for `multitoken` name. However I would move this django app to seprate project to not mess djoser core functionality.

When we add support for other auth backends (see issues) there is no reason to keep `multitoken` as a part of `djoser`. What's more I can imagine that we could provide more features to `multitoken` app like some storage or caching backends. This will be not connected with djoser directly and it can end up with mess in pull requests and issues on github.

**Authentication process**
In general it totally makes sense what you did with `TokenAuthentication` class in this new `token` app. However considering issues #28 and #40 I would suggest the new way of handling auth backends in djoser. Currently we have a lot of code depending on `TokenAuthentication`. Probably it's not good idea if we want to support other backends.

That's why I would remove all the code depending on `TokenAuthentication`, especially `UserLoginSerializer` and `LoginView` classes. With the new `token` app the responsibility how the authentication is handled should rely on this new app, not on `djoser`. It's handled exactly in the same way by other auth backends:

* [JWT auth](https://github.com/GetBlimp/django-rest-framework-jwt/blob/master/rest_framework_jwt/views.py#L40),
* [Token auth from DRF](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/3.1.2/rest_framework/authtoken/views.py#L9),
* and what's funny `BasicAuthentication` backend does not require any obtain view.

**Other  stuff**
I agree we should remove `LOGIN_AFTER_ACTIVATION` / `LOGIN_AFTER_REGISTRATION` settings and support.

**Further steps**
Here is what I suggest to do now:

1. Extract new token app as a new project
2. Close this PR and branch.
3. Create new branch when we add support for other auth backend including new multitoken app.
4. When the all above works we will merge it to djoser and release new major version.

Hi @leferaycloud.
Firstly - sorry it took so long for me to respond.

I believe that you may have confused the REST architecture with a traditional single layer. In djoser we expect that your backend is used to provide API for mobile apps or your frontend layer (e.g. Single Page App) and therefore the process you have described is not something we had in mind with djoser.

Although - what you say is possible I'm not sure why you would need REST API if you wish to handle everything within the single Django layer. Here is how you would usually handle user activation:

1. User creates his account via POST to `users/create/`.
2. User receives email with URL to frontend activation page defined via [ACTIVATION_URL](http://djoser.readthedocs.io/en/latest/settings.html#activation-url) djoser setting.
3. User heads to the given page, which calls the `users/activate/` with POST with `uid` and `token`, which indeed you extract from the URL GET params.

If for whatever reason you want to handle everything within Django, my first thought would be to use *requests* to call the djoser activation URL, but as I said it's not how you would usually want to do this.

Hope this answers your question,

Previously, xpconanfan (Ang Li) wrote…
I see, does this mean we should log this based on the number given back by the message 'SNIPPET START, PROTOCOL x x' ?
Actually that wouldn't be accurate. The protocol is a negotiation between the server and client, but ultimately the client decides the protocol to use to start the app. The log message being discussed here is logging the client's final decision, not what the server advertised. For example suppose we add protocol 1.1 which adds a 'PING' message where the server replies 'PONG' and use this for a heartbeat. This is backwards compatible. The server would advertise 1.1, but until we modify the client code, we'd still be using 1.0 to bring it up. So it would be incorrect to say we're bringing up the snippet using protocol 1.1 until we actually adjust the client code to make use of the new protocol features. 1.0 would be the right thing to log here. --- *Comments from [Reviewable](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/253)*

Oh, and I think I understand why this was hitting the Sersic but not b+d models:

- Sersic models were transformed by shearing etc.  So they were actually ```galsim.Transformation```s, with an ```original``` attribute already.  Adding an ```index``` to them didn't help, because when they got further transformed by the lensing shear, their already-present ```original``` attribute was copied over, and it didn't have an ```index```.  That's why my latest commit fixed those.
- B+D models were compound/sum objects.  Each component had been transformed, but then they were added together.  The sum doesn't have an ```original``` attribute, but it had been getting an ```index```.  So, when the sums went into a later shearing operations, they didn't have an ```original``` attribute yet, and the final ```galsim.Transformation``` ended up with the compound object (which did have an ```index```) as its ```original``` attribute.

Anyway, since I have talked to myself enough, I will summarize the questions that I think are left for @rmjarvis -

The particular design image suggested for the tiles design cannot represent a typical treemap: it is neither arranged by sorting (the largest block would be first in the top left corner), nor stable and optimized for aspect ratio (the largest block would be aligned to the right edge), but it is perfectly valid for a generic "tiles" layout.

I found issue which is very specific to the way I implemented. I use 'signal' module to monitor if the redis fetch is completed within 10 secs. If the redis does not give response within 10 sec (which sometimes it does not because i fetch GBs of data in one query), I just raise exception and then catch it to bypass that code execution and move on to next query.

Now, when the execution is normal and function is not taking 10 sec, everything works well. If any query takes more than 10 sec and exception is raised, the hiredis implementation leaks that memory. It does not reclaim the memory while normal redis comes back to normal usage. 

Does this explanation ring any bell? My dataset is very simple. I store around 15kb of binary data with 'SET' method against a key. I have around 0.3 million such key-value pairs.

I think it is not dataset but my implementation specific. I should also mention I run everything inside docker containers.

> Which mode would be the preferred one?

Endpoints. Otherwise there is a race condition: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/issues/536

> I noticed that it is important to clean up. If you install it and an old endpoint or configmap still exists, the cluster will form correctly, but the service doesn't get the endpoint. Maybe this can be improved.

I don't think that it will "form correctly". Patroni will notice that there is a `<cluster-name>-config` endpoint or configmap, assume that cluster was already initialized and will not run initdb but try to restore from a backup (with wal-e) or all pods wait until there will be a leader to get basebackup from it.

Database clusters are stateful and usually you don't delete-create them all the time. There is not much we can improve.


> I also experimented with parallel pod management and rolling updates. Do you see a problem with this?

I've never played with rolling upgrades so far, but I think it might be dangerous. It is very important to not terminate the next pod until the previous one become healthy enough (started streaming from master and replication lag close to 0). 
Actually we are not really using helm charts, but [postgres operator](https://github.com/zalando-incubator/postgres-operator) for everything: cluster creation and removal, user management, PV management, rolling upgrades and so on.

So, I looked into the Env Vars. The reason it works with `pip install .` and not with `pip install openfisca-core` is because `. ` is a python project, and `openfisca-core` is a [wheel](http://wheel.readthedocs.io/en/stable/).
When you `pip install` a python project, `os.environ` in `setup.py` is the *user*'s environment.
When you `pip install` a wheel, `os.environ`in `setup.py` is the *wheel builder*'s environment.
The wheels figure out all the dependencies when it's built.

There is a way to pass env vars, but very specific `sys` variables to have conditionnal dependencies such as `python version`, or `os`.

Anywho, in my mind, we have two options : 
A/ Change to `openfisca-core[web]` to install the web-api dependencies. (Web is more meaningful in my mind than API, because we also have a python api)
B/ have a NO_API env var, to be used with `pip install .`

Looking forward to have your opinion on this :)

The NEXRAD Level 3 reader does not do support loading directly from a tar file, nor do I think it should. Trying to support inline unpacking/decompression of various archive formats get complicated fast and can be handled easily outside the read function.  I'll add support for reading from a file-like object so that it will be possible to open the file with the standard library **tarfile** module and pass a member to the `read_nexrad_level3` function.  You can already do the same with most (all?) of the other file types supported by Py-ART.  

NEXRAD Level 2 supports reading directly from bzip2 files as some service was serving files in that format (NCDC) and it was simple enough to add and the **bz2** module was already being imported.  In hindsight it probably would have been better to refer users to the **bz2** module and have them pass a **BZ2File** to the `read_nexrad_level2` to be more inline with other read functions.

Finally it should be mentioned that `pyart.io.read` will attempt to perform inline decompression of gzip and bzip2 files.  This cannot be done for archive files (tar, zip) as they can contain multiple files.  Passing a the file-like handler will work for these using the **tarfile** or** zipfile** modules.

@mikesjqubit Thanks for the report.  `gcloud.bigquery.query.Query.fetch_data` actually returns data in "pages", with a page token.  In the second example under the [Querying data (synchronous)](http://googlecloudplatform.github.io/gcloud-python/stable/bigquery-usage.html#querying-data-synchronous) usage docs, note the loop over pages:

```python
query.run()
do_something_with(query.schema, query.rows)
token = query.page_token  # for initial request
while True:
    do_something_with(query.schema, rows)
    if token is None:
        break
    rows, total_count, token = query.fetch_data(page_token=token)
```

Right, pipeline code is expected be idempotent in both cases. I am not
certain about the Python implementation but the Java implementation is
slightly more lenient in the "yield the same results and the same
side-effects" part. Basically, it is true that the same Job can run more
than once but once run the job state will be changed conditionally and
atomically (CAS). Such a change (including adding child job) will be
dropped if job state was already modified. This means that as long as your
jobs do not change external state/data you should be OK and not experience
any data clobbering in case input changes between runs.

OK, I don't know where else to put this, so I'm putting it here. I've struggled mightily to figure out this API and I hope this feedback will be helpful. Two things:

1. To set the `lifecycle_rules`, you need to set them before you create the bucket:

        b = client.bucket('my-bucket-name')
        b.exists()  # False
        b.lifecycle_rules = [{
            'action': {'type': 'Delete'},
            'condition': {'age': 1},
        }]
        b.create()

    It doesn't seem to be possible to set lifecycle rules for an existing bucket, though I'd love to be proven wrong. If this *is* possible, it's certainly not documented anywhere I could find. I'd expect that a setter would actually set the values on in Google Cloud or that there would be a `commit` command somewhere to commit the changes (I haven't found this...yet). Since neither is the case, maybe setting these values on an existing bucket should throw an exception? 

2. After you've created a bucket like this, you can test that the creation worked by using the `reload` method on a bucket:

        b = client.bucket('my-bucket-name')
        b.lifecycle_rules  # Returns empty list: [], WTF?
        b.reload()
        b.lifecycle_rules  # Returns the rules from above. Success!

    Since `reload` is *also* undocumented, this is *extremely* difficult to figure out, since for all intents and purposes, it looks like your lifecycle rules didn't get created when you check them. The only thing that tipped me off was that there didn't seem to be any network latency when I was trying to get the rules. If I had been on a faster connection, this would have been even harder to detect. 

    Do you want another ticket for documenting the `reload` function? I'm new to Google Cloud, but this whole thing is extremely frustrating. There are some big gaps here in documentation and user expectation.

I saw that discussion. I don't use multiprocessing. I don't think there is threading, but just in case, I make sure all my clients are thread-local using the function below.

Perhaps I should have mentioned, but this call is being made across projects. It is being made from an instance within a project named `ocr-api`, but is trying to grab data from a project named `docketupdate`. 

Also, I have a cloud support package, and I can try opening a case if that would help.

```
import threading
_datastore_client = threading.local()
def datastore_client(project = None):
    '''Return a thread-unique datastore client for the given project.'''
    # Our global datstore client cache.
    global _datastore_client
    if not getattr(_datastore_client, 'clients'):
        _datastore_client.clients = {}
    if project not in _datastore_client.clients:
        # Create a new datastore client and save it in the cache.
        _datastore_client.clients[project] = datastore.client.Client(project) \
            if project else datastore.client.Client()
    return _datastore_client.clients[project]
```

Yah, 731d124 fixes a related problem where --cache-ttl is set and still raises Unsatisfiable. If there's no --cache-ttl then you can still hit this because it returns 3.2.1 from cache because it's exact and then searches pypi for the unbounded and takes the latest and those don't match in the `resolvable_set.merge()`. Since pants always sets --cache-ttl I fixed it for that case.

So, regarding diving into the `tokio` dep. It is a relatively large and complicated dependency, but it also has very high velocity, and seems to be where most of the interesting async work in the community is being done.

Packages that we would be able to use from tokio:
* `tokio-process`: Allows for managing forked processes without parent threads
* `tokio-fs`: Supports "async file IO", which could be used to replace our synchronous consumption of the filesystem on a threadpool
* `tokio-timer`: Would support our timeout/retry usecases (again, without separate threads)
* `tokio-threadpool`: The support infrastructure that allows `tokio-fs` to do "async file IO" (ie, the `blocking` facility) also allows for marking other operations as potentially blocking, meaning that we could avoid context switches for accessing `LMDB` if we annotated them as blocking.

But a con for this is that looking at the docs last night, it was not (yet, most likely) obvious how to use the `blocking` API outside the context of creating custom futures. But I expect that this is a short term issue, as more example of this new feature will come around, and the API is likely to become easier to use.

Finally, if we decide that we want to do this, it doesn't have to be a big-bang swapover, as demonstrated by the [branch I started](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/compare/master...twitter:stuhood/tokio), which moves work off of the main thread and onto the tokio runtime (but does not yet move any of the other usecases).

Note that this is all prep work for rewriting the `PythonEval` task to use new pipeline constructs. It won't use the interpreter selected by the interpreter selection task (as that selects one for all targets in the closure, while `PythonEval` needs to select separately for each target it evals), so the `--constraints` option needs to be somewhere independent of either task, and `PythonSetup` is where all other interpreter selection options go.

We can move `--constraints` without deprecation because the new python pipeline has not appeared in any release.

I believe that I have a design for an overhaul of Variants (and Singletons... and Subjects) that I'm pretty excited about.

The general idea is that rather than every Node having a subject, every Node will instead have a set/map of subjects (a map from type to value). Similar to how the rulegraph statically determines which subject type is valid in each subgraph (by recursing to determine whether a requested product type is computable given a subject and all installed singletons), in this case the rulegraph would determine which _set_ of subject types is required in a subgraph.

All available subject values would be stored in the `Context` for a running Node, and the rulegraph would determine which subjects to incorporate into each Node's identity. Importantly (and satisfying the constraints in the description): subjects which the rule graph statically shows are not relevant to a subgraph will _not_ become part of the `Node`'s identity. This means that the set of subjects relevant to any given Node might be "zero or more" (which has the neat effect of allowing for parameterless rules... _true_ singletons).

----

I'm very excited about this for a few reasons:
1. It accomplishes the goal in the description of avoiding including Variants in Node identities where they aren't relevant.
2. It replaces Singletons with a more powerful concept.
3. Because it is 100% type driven, we can remove `SelectVariant` and Select, and simply have `@rule` parameters always be types from the subject set. 

Some of the credit for this idea is due to @cosmicexplorer, who when getting started writing `@rule`s thought that things _already_ worked this way. So yea... maybe they can!

While I don't get a stack trace, it still feels confusing. An obvious thing to do (to me), if you're starting off with pants is something like this:

```
[drift pants (master)]$ ./pants compile --help
...
  --[no-]compile-java-warnings
  --[no-]warnings         Compile with all configured warnings enabled.
                          (default: True)
...
```

You see `--[no-]warnings` in the help, and think "cool, let's disable the warnings":

```
[drift pants (master)]$ ./pants compile examples/src/java/com/pants/examples/hello/greet --no-warnings

Exception message: unrecognized arguments: --no-warnings

```

I think at a minimum, we could improve this error message to include a link to a doc that explains short vs. long arguments. It might also be possible to go a bit farther, and have pants spit out something that says:

```
Sorry, the argument '--no-warnings' is either ambiguous or doesn't exist. Did you mean:
  --[no-]compile-scala-warnings
  --[no-]compile-apt-warnings
  --[no-]compile-java-warnings
```

I have no idea how hard it would be to implement that, but I think it would pants a lot more understandable for newcomers (and would even be nice for old-timers).

I have to claim TL;DR; on your post, but I think this is what you're after:

The `.text` property replaces all the paragraphs in a cell, taking any paragraph or cell-level formatting with it. If you want to preserve the formatting, you need to work at the run level. In this case I think you want to add runs to an existing paragraph, so something like:

```python
paragraph = cell.paragraphs[0]  # there will always be at least one, even if cell is empty
run = paragraph.add_run(...)
run.text = "new text"
```

In case of XML above, the "center" property is on the paragraph. Since you preserve the paragraph, that formatting is preserved. If it were italics or something and that formatting was applied at the run level, you'd have to get a handle for the particular run and manipulate the text inside it to preserve its formatting.

@vruusmann

What version of JPMML are you using?

First, the conversion from `double` to `float` was not available in older versions, because it loses numeric precision. However, it has been enabled since September 2015 (eg. see https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-evaluator/commit/2079ebc0d6ec7c52e62eb643f406d123ac48d892).

Please note that SkLearn uses 32-bit floating-point values for representing tree split conditions. Therefore, it is absolutely necessary to use the same datatype in PMML also, because otherwise some splits may be evaluated incorrectly.

Second, you're working with a classification-type problem, where final (ie. top-level `MiningModel` element) class probability distribution is calculated by applying the `average` aggregation function over all member (ie. nested `TreeModel` elements) class probability distributions. JPMML uses interface `org.jpmml.evaluator.HasProbability` to expose that information to interested parties.

Both of your problems can be solved by upgrading to the latest JPMML-Evaluator library version, which is `1.2.11` at the moment. Also, the upgrade should give you a considerable performance boost.

This is a legitimate bug now. The derived field `x3` depends on the user-supplied field `Petal.Length`, but the latter is not "imported" by the `MiningSchema` element of the `TreeModel` element.

Probably, this happens because your `DataFrameMapper` object does not specify any transformations for input fields.

No matter which framework we choose, we'll probably still have some options around how much we want to tune the performance at the cost of introducing additional logic. For example,

* With Backbone+Mn we can re-render the entire DOM tree on every single event, or we can tell certain sub-views to only listen to certain model update events.
* With React we could leave `shouldComponentUpdate` in the default `true` and diff the entire virtual dom, or we can implement `shouldComponentUpdate` to be a bit smarter and only diff certain pieces.

However if we compare the naive version of both, diff-ing the whole virtual DOM is almost guaranteed to be faster re-rendering the whole real DOM.

One other consideration is that re-rendering portions of the DOM can introduce issues when the user is interacting with the elements. One classic example is putting a red validation border on an email input box. If the border is shown by re-rendering the `<input>` element (as opposed to just updating its `class` attribute), the users's cursor position and focus will be lost. Traditionally the way around this would be to use something like JQuery to change the styling, but now the view is no longer stateless and purely declarative. A virtual DOM (or something that behaves similarly) would be able to update the styling without destroying the `<input>` element.

It's certainly interesting that your locale's encoding appears to be set to None. This message is a warning rather than an error; its purpose is to inform users with a non-UTF8 encoding that they may encounter errors running in the command line if their locale encoding isn't set to utf-8.

This would be harmless in the event that you do not receive utf-8 data, but can cause unexpected errors otherwise.

charm build supports an 'ignores' keyword in the layers config. This is a
list following the gitignore std pattern. In fact build should be ignoring
things that are in your .gitignore already. What either you or charm build
has backwards is that currently the layer above the layer your 1 off test
is in is what makes the choice to include it or not. Rather than a "should
I push this forward during merge" mentality the thinking is, if it is in
the layer it is there for a reason, I am only one possible consumer of that
layer. If that layer wants to ignore those tests that is its business.

This is totally ill advised. You will not get a 'done' message fast enough, therefore your select will return nothing, and later on, the first command will be acked, and you will read one 'done'.
Over time, a number of message will accumulate on the pipe and things will break as you see.

You do not need to ACK the "done" message before sending the next command. We ack the message in the order the command were sent.

2. Use this method to collect assignments instructions: https://www.coursera.org/api/onDemandProgrammingLearnerAssignments.v1/01XLylXyEeWKXg4Y7_tPaw~UzPbw?fields=submissionLearnerSchema
3. Collect all `elements[*].submissionLearnerSchema.definition.assignmentInstructions.definition.value` and extract all `asset` tags. Attributes `id`, `name`, `extension` are useful.
4. Obtain direct links by calling https://www.coursera.org/api/assetUrls.v1?ids=yeJ7Q8VAEeWPRQ4YsSEORQ, grab all `elements[*].url`.
5. [optional] If you use direct `url` from `assertUrls` request, filename is ugly, it contains some hash `_3cdeda0745801a9686d7f5601010fb0c_statement-pca.pdf`. A better way to prettify it is to match its `id` with ids collected in step 3, and use `name` and `extension` attributes from there.

I didn't look too closely at the code, just the API.  Adding stateful functionality is great, but adding the `stacked` option breaks the `Seq2SeqEncoder` API.  By definition, a `Seq2SeqEncoder` takes inputs of shape `(batch_size, sequence_length, input_dim)` and returns outputs of shape `(batch_size, sequence_length, output_dim)`.  Returning stacked outputs doesn't fit that definition, and makes it so that `Seq2SeqEncoders` are not interchangeable - you'd have to change your code to switch encoders.

I definitely agree that returning stacked outputs is something you might want to do, we just need to do it outside of a `Seq2SeqEncoder`.  In fact, if you want to do that, you're probably just better off using a pytorch RNN, instead of a `Seq2SeqEncoder`.  That abstraction only exists to allow you to abstract away how you go from `(batch_size, sequence_length, input_dim)` to `(batch_size, sequence_length, output_dim)` - if you're not doing that operation, or don't need to abstract away the _how_, then you don't need to use the abstraction.

Hi

The problem lies in your implementation of `__eq__` I'm riffraff. The way that operator overloading works in python for operators that take two arguments (binary operators) is that the relevant special method (aka magic method or dunder method) is called on one of the objects, if that object doesn't now how to do the operation for the supplied operands it signals this by returning `NotImplemented` so the corresponding method is then called on the other object.

More details about this can be found at:

https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#the-standard-type-hierarchy

and

https://docs.python.org/3/library/numbers.html#implementing-the-arithmetic-operations

In your example `__eq__` is being called on an instance of `Model` passing in an instance of `Any` as the other object. Your code doesn't know how to handle this situation and doesn't return `NotImplemented` so the `__eq__` on `Any` never gets a chance to have a go.

It is normal for implementations of operators such as `__eq__` to test the type of the other operand to check whether the operation is implemented. So in your example you should implement the `Model` class as follows:

    class Model(object):
        def __init__(self, id):
            self.id = id

        def __eq__(self, other):
            if isinstance(other, Model):
                return self.id == other.id`
            else:
                return NotImplemented

I hope this helps

I'm just sort of musing/rambling here... but, for what it's worth...

I agree with the plan in general. I think we could make a few architectural commitments early on that would make life easier over the long run.

**On stderr/stdout:** In general, I agree with your sentiments about not testing output so much as return values. But I think there are also very valid cases for capturing and testing the output. And for that reason, I bet there's a fairly simple alteration we could make that would improve overall design.

I feel it'll be in our best interest to have most of the code write to an `io.Writer` instead of `os.Stdout`, and have the main function pass in os.Stdout/os.Stderr. Long term, that makes it much easier to use code as a library. (Possibly better is to use `log.Logger` and write things to that. The problem with log.Logger is that it doesn't make it easy to write error content to a different writer than non-error content.)

So in the current design, main() would call the runner like this:

```go
os.Exit(Command(argv []string, os.Stdin, os.Stdout)
```

Tests would be able to conveniently capture stdin/stdout data by simply passing in a buffer.Buffer instead of stdout/stderr. You can think of this as sort of simple dependency injection: The outermost environment determines which dependencies inner functions use.

(I am a huge fan of using the io Reader/Writer abstractions whenever possible because you get a ton of flexibility.)

Along these lines, I think we need to get rid of the outchan/errchan channel thing. A writer is a more appropriate thing here than a channel, and honestly we're losing flexibility with the channel handling.

**On mocking vs. stubbing:** One of the cool things about Go is that you can define an interface against an existing API, which has the benefit of making it easy to write very specific stubs/mocks.

Say you have an external library with 20 methods, but you only need to use a few. Define an interface for it, and use that interface in your code. Then, when you go to write tests, you can write mocks against the interface instead of re-implementing the entire external library.

So say our imaginary 3rd party lib has a struct called `SpellChecker`. SpellChecker has, among it's many methods, a Check method that we want to use. That one function checks a string and returns a bool saying whether it's spelled right. That's the only thing we use out of that struct. We can define an interface for this:

```go
type checker interface {
  Check(string) bool
}

// Instead of DoCheck(s string, *c SpellChecker)
func DoCheck(s string, c checker) {
}
```

Then when we write our tests, we just need to mock a `checker`. Of course, the scary part about using interfaces is that you're not supposed to change public interfaces once they're defined. So this is a strategy you have to employ thoughtfully and strategically.

>  I don't like the idea of depth -1 for infinite. Too easy to get hung up on loops in the filesystem "tree".

I'll leave a depth of `-1` off the menu for now. You can always give us a depth of `1000` or something similarly absurd.
I was considering the possibility of having unlimited depth as an "at your own risk" kind of option, but after some thought I agree that it's not really worth it.

> I'm fine with --recursive-depth-limit though as noted it is somewhat verbose. Assume that providing this without the corresponding --recursive is considered erroneous input (simply --recursive N with an optional N seems nice, but ambiguous if the last flag assuming there are default arguments that are not preceded with a --flag (should --path behave that way (i.e. we simply provide the path(s) as the final argument(s) to the ls command?)).

Although I would like `--recursive N` for this particular use-case, the feature-reject in `click` for this exact kind of behavior has some salient points about how this hurts the extensibility and learnability of the CLI: https://github.com/pallets/click/issues/370

Basically, it's the exact point you raised of positional arguments becoming ambiguous.

Potentially confusing behavior is leading me to avoid positional arguments as much as possible.
For example, how do you read `globus transfer async-transfer <uuid1> <uuid2> <uuid3> <uuid4>`? Which UUID is the source endpoint ID, and which one is the destination path?
Passing `--path` for `ls` may seem silly today, but future features may render that clarity really useful.

> All output paths/names (either TEXT or JSON) should complete the complete path in the tree (at least relative to --path)

I'm a little confused by this.
For JSON output, I assume it means you want to be able to join the `--path` you supplied with the filenames produced, and not traverse some kind of nested structure.

However, for TEXT, do you mean you want output similar to `find <dirname>`, rather than `ls -R <dirname>`?
The `find` output is actually a lot easier to work with, IMO, and even simpler for me to produce, but I'm not sure that it's the right behavior -- I don't know if `ls` should behave as much like the coreutils `ls` as it reasonably can, or if it should just try to be reasonable/useful.

>and I see now that these aren't really intended to be transactions but batching some subset of changes

Exactly. I elaborated further on this [here](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/569#discussion_r183639713)

So, let me elaborate a bit further on the part that I find to be the most unsound about this change. Especially as it is very subtle and may have been overlooked. 

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/569/files#diff-d71f9bfe38d29edaf17d6231470873ccR118

Notice that this PR changes `self.db` on the `ChainDB` to be the non-wrapped underlying db and moves the wrapped `JournalDB` to a new property `self.journal_db`. 

So why is that? It's because the new `JournalDB` does not write anything back to the underlying db by default (until one calls `persist()`). However, there are certain places in `ChainDB` that do some changes to the db and also do not need journaling or batching. In order for these places to work one would have to do something like this:

```python
db.set()
db.persist()
```

But here's the problem. We have tests where the chaindb uses a `NonJournaledAsyncChainDB` that entirely bypasses the `JournalDB` so that there is no `persist()` available. Where the `NonJournaledAsyncChainDB` is used, there also is no transaction processing so it seemed *safe* to simply change all these places to bypass the `JournalDB` by default and only use the `JournalDB`  for the places that really need the journaling functionality.

While that works to get the test suite to pass I'm not sure if it is really really sound or this actually introduces undesired behavior that our test suite simply does not catch yet.

In addition to that, I feel like this change lowered the cohesion of the `ChainDB` and I was wondering about possible alternatives. However, in the light of #507 and as you already said, this may just be good enough for now?

The relative path here just gets converted to an absolute path `file://` url.  Which also means explicit tarball installs from `https://` urls work just fine too.

> We may want to add a third option that forces conda to consider only the highest priority channel where the package exists

I like this idea. Thus, `config.channel_priority` would become three valued, e.g. `highest`, `ordered`, and `equal`?

Anaconda 5 no longer supports CentOS 5 / glibc 2.5 as the baseline.  It has moved to CentOS 6 / glibc 2.12.  You'll need to stick with conda < 4.3.27, which will keep you on the old "defaults" channel.  The new defaults channel includes https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main, and this is where all software will be compatible with glibc 2.12 and up.

Below is the current draft for the help string.  Definitely interested in getting feedback on the examples, and the capabilities they are demonstrating.

```
Key parts of conda's functionality require that it interact directly with the shell 
within which conda is being invoked. The `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` commands 
specifically are shell-level commands. That is, they affect the state (e.g. environment 
variables) of the shell context being interacted with. Other core commands, like 
`conda create` and `conda install`, also necessarily interact with the shell environment. 
They're therefore implemented in ways specific to each shell. Each shell must be configured 
to make use of them.

Examples:
    
    # Make the conda command available in all detected shells for the *current user*.
    # For example, if the bash shell is among those detected on your system, this command
    # will modify your user's `~/.bashrc` file (`~/.bash_profile` on macOS). In addition to,
    # the 'conda' command being made available, the 'base' conda environment is automatically
    # activated (i.e. environment variables set and all executables put on PATH).
    
        $ conda init
    
    # Make the conda command available to every user on the system. For example, if the bash 
    # shell is among those detected on your system, a file `/etc/profile.d/conda.sh` will be
    # created, which will be sourced when your shell is initialized.
    
        $ conda init --system
    
    # Initialize conda in all detected shells for the current user. Additionally, make *only*
    # the 'conda' command available to the shell, and *do not* automatically activate the 
    # base environment. Executables in the base environment, like python, will not be 
    # available on PATH until running 'conda activate base'.
    
        $ conda init --no-auto-activate
    
    # When executed from a bash shell, ensure that conda is properly installed in the prefix 
    # for the first python on PATH (e.g. following a `python -m pip install conda`). Make the 
    # 'conda' command available to the current shell process, and activate the 'base' 
    # environment. Does not modify `~/.bashrc` for example, so has no effect on future shell
    # sessions.
    
        $ eval `python -m conda init --install-only bash`


IMPORTANT: After running `conda init`, most shells will need to be closed and restarted
           for changes to take effect.
```

In addition to these examples, there will be one more not included here, but included in developer documentation somewhere (those dev docs really don't yet exist).  That example might look like the following

```
# An example for creating an environment to develop on conda's own code. Clone the 
# conda repo and install a dedicated miniconda within it. Remove all remnants of 
# conda source files in the `site-packages` directory associated with 
# `~/conda/devenv/bin/python`. Write a `conda.pth` file in that `site-packages` 
# directory pointing to source code in `~/conda`, the current working directory.
# Write commands to stdout, suitable for bash `eval`, that sets up the current
# shell as a dev environment. 
    
    $ git clone git@github.com:conda/conda ~/conda
    $ curl -L https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -o ~/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
    $ bash ~/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -bfp ~/conda/devenv
    $ cd ~/conda
    $ eval `./devenv/bin/python -m conda init --dev bash`

```

That's exactly the kind of rules that I would like to see in writing so that people don't have to reverse-engineer them. Thus, your rule would be:
* If a package comes in several variants, the feature-free variant is preferred, unless overridden by an active 'track_features' in the current environment.

This is reasonable, but doesn't cover the case when a package has only featured variants. Will this result in an 'ambiguous feature resolution' error (with suitable hints to install a 'track_features' package first), or will package developers have the possibility to specify the default choice explicitly? If the second, would it be reasonable to allow other packages to override the 'feature-free is preferred' default as well?

Moreover, what are the implications of three-stage optimization in more difficult situations? One hot topic of discussion at conda-forge is how to have pypi-derived recipes in which the metadata is readily updatable by pulling in current information from PyPI.  The current way to do things is to either 1) grab an existing recipe and modify it by hand; or 2) use ```conda skeleton pypi```, and then modify the skeleton by hand, to add things like the conda-forge maintainer list, and strip out comments.

Conda-build has good support for Jinja2 templating, which certainly allows for any dynamic recipes you can dream up, but we're missing an implementation of how to use that for having more dynamic metadata (recomputed/rediscovered at build time).  Beyond this, automatic mirroring is little more than monitoring package release RSS feeds, or something similar.  There's a lot of interest in that kind of thing at conda-forge.  Please raise this issue at conda-forge's staged-recipes issue tracker - I'm not sure it is something we have as an issue yet.  We have discussed it a fair bit in meetings.  Our meeting notes, agendas, and schedule are at https://conda-forge.hackpad.com/conda-forge-meetings-2YkV96cvxPG

```
/path/to/conda/envs/myenv/bin/activate: line 86: [: =: unary operator expected`
```
Unfortunately, I don't believe this can be fixed without some kind of db or file-system level semaphore - since this looks like a race between 2 instances where one is in python and the other is in bash.

I found that since the parallel jobs in my clustered env only want to run python and not any conda commands, simply prepending PATH is sufficient for my needs.

So, it seems that activating an environment is simply not a re-entrant operation at the moment *but* just setting PATH is enough for "activating" execution-only environments.  This seems something well worth documenting.

I see some other details that might also go against what I'd consider best practices for post-link scripts.  We should really right up something for a docs about what we recommend as best practices for post-link, how to use it correctly, and different things we've seen that we consider anti-patterns.  I'll add that to the long TODO list :)

`CONDA_ENV_PATH` holds either the environment name, or a path to the environment.  `CONDA_PREFIX` is guaranteed to hold the full path to the active environment. (At least as of conda 4.1.)

>  Can I rely on the subdirectory named 'conda-meta' being present, along with the current structure of the .json files in there?

Yes!  To detect if a directory *is* a conda prefix, you can test of the existence of a `conda-meta/history` file.  As to the structure of the json files in the conda-meta directory, any change other than pure additions will go through a well-documented an lengthy migration plan.  But I really don't foresee any real breaking changes at any point on the horizon.  Only additions to what's already there.

I guess at some point it makes sense to start using a `version` key within those json files, just to be safe.  I'll add that next time we make a change in that area.  Still, the first will be a simple addition to what's already there.

Hi tylerlong,

I had the same issue on Windows 7.  I first attempted to install each pip package, and if installed, then I updated it.

Try (outside of Python on command prompt):
```
python -m pip install pubnub
python -m pip install requests
python -m pip install pubnub --update
python -m pip install requests  --update
python -m pip install ringcentral
```

That cleared up the issue for me.  I hope this is helpful for you and others.  

Cheers.
Michael

If you note the line above, it's actually accurate:

```
Starting new HTTPS connection (1): localhost
```
The python Elasticsearch module is logging in a way that is less than 100% informative is why it says "http" instead of "https", but it _is_ connecting via HTTPS (or at least attempting to).

I see that you have `--certificate /etc/mycert`.  What exactly is in that file?  Is that a directory?  Curator requires a full path to an actual certificate (usually PEM formatted).  The `--certificate` flag is for a certificate like the ones that ship with a browser, so that Curator can verify that the SSL keys on the server side match what is expected.  If you've made a self-signed certificate, you'd likely use the CA certificate (not key) here.

As a side note, are you using an older version of Elasticsearch?  Why are you still using 3.5.1?

It doesn't just add a 1, it adds a 1 to the timestamp and a `%d` to the timestring:

`timestring` becomes: `%Y.%m` -> `%Y.%m%d`
and
`index_timestamp` becomes: `2017.02` -> `2017.021`

```
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> timestring = '%Y.%m'
>>> index_timestamp = '2017.02'
>>> datetime.strptime(index_timestamp, timestring)
datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 1, 0, 0)
>>> timestring += '%d'
>>> index_timestamp += '1'
>>> datetime.strptime(index_timestamp, timestring)
datetime.datetime(2017, 2, 1, 0, 0)
```

This admittedly silly workaround was added to support older versions of Python, as without it, some crufty old versions of datetime didn't parse it properly (I don't remember the PR that fixed this issue).  

In any case, it doesn't change the result in any way, so it shouldn't be acting as a block to you.

Thanks @tjprescott,

I applied your recommendations, and in addition, I had to remove the `resource_group` elements.
With all these changes, the json was parsed successfully.

But then my journey was not over, so for sake of completeness, here are the different steps i had to do in order to "Add a second IpConfiguration to an existing virtual scale set"

Closing the ticket.
---

1.  I had to use the command `az resource update` instead of `az vmss update` in order to use the latest API version (required to be able to add a second IpConfiguration).
1.1. using `az resource update` required the json to be changed, because the resources are nested in 
`properties` elements
2. I had to flag the existing IpConfiguration as 'Primary', this could be achieved with the following command `az resource update --id '/subscriptions/subscriptionId/resourceGroups/resourceGroupName/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets/vmssName'  --resource-group 'resourceGroupName' --set properties.virtualMachineProfile.networkProfile.networkInterfaceConfigurations[0].properties.ipConfigurations[0].properties primary=true`
3. Then i tried to use `az resource update`, but this time it complained with `subscription is not registered for feature Microsoft.Network/AllowPublicIpsAndLoadBalancingOnSecondaryIpConfigsInVMScaleSet required to carry out the requested operation.`
4. I could add the feature with this command  `az feature register --name 'AllowPublicIpsAndLoadBalancingOnSecondaryIpConfigsInVMScaleSet' --namespace 'Microsoft.Network'`

Thanks,

I think there should be a better way to do this but I'll tell you how I currently do this and give some additional ideas and then a feature idea :)

### abusing hook stages

Note: I personally don't use pre-push so if I don't want a hook to run normally I configure it as follows:

```yaml
    -   id: dont-run-normally
        stages: [push]
```

That way during `commit` it doesn't normally run.  However when I want to manually run it I can do: `pre-commit run dont-run-normally --hook-stage push` to make it run.

(woops! turns out `--hook-stage` is undocumented on [pre-commit.com](https://pre-commit.com/#pre-commit-run)!)

### try-repo

Another idea is to use ]`pre-commit try-repo /path/to/repo`](https://pre-commit.com/#pre-commit-try-repo), probably not the best when you have to remember the incantation each time though :)

### rubyvenv

For ruby specifically, I've been slowly (haven't really made progress in a long time) been trying to factor out the pre-commit ruby support into a separate package (also so I can attempt to tackle #201 (the oldest as-of-now open issue)).  There's an incomplete, but for a lot of usecases works fine "ruby virtualenv"ish tool: https://github.com/asottile/rubyvenv -- it needs a lot of polishing, needs to support _building_ ruby, and still needs windows support somehow but otherwise is functional with prebuilt rubies.

### how I think this should work -- abusing hook stages for greater good!

Checking this scenario I could see that the simple execution of `set_exception` will create a new reference for the specific `Future` involved with the loop, because of the call soon [1]. Hence, the destructor of the `Future` won't be called. IMHO this is the behavior that I will expect, the same for the happy path `set_result`. Therefore, the explicit close of the TCPconnector should not raise this log entry in execution time.

True that once the program finishes this messages might be printed if the exception is not gathered by the caller, but IMHO this is or it should be the default behavior.

[1] https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/asyncio/futures.py#L129

Another pain point, which I don't know if it could be addressed for the 4.x series is the fact that certain nbextensions depend on (a certain version) kernel-side code such as custom interactive widgets libraries.

At the moment, it is impossible to have two kernels with two different versions of ipywidgets, or bqplot, installed because they will look for the JavaScript at the same location.

Therefore, I think that the extension mechanism should acknowledge that there are two categories of extensions
 1. Global notebook application extensions, which is not dependent on kernel code.
 2. Kernel-dependent extensions.

A proposal to solve this would be to have the kernelspec contain one more information: a uuid which would typically be generated when the kernelspec is installed. When running a notebook with this kernel, `nbextension_base_path/u-u-i-d/` would then become a search path, and the one we use for custom widgets.

+1, big fan of `entry_points` as the least-worst solution for extensibility, especially with the non-setuptools version by @takluyver, and a win from a development, packaging, documentation, user experience point of view.

Presumably, we'd just duplicate part of the traitlets config namespaces:
```python
setup(
    # ...
    entry_points={
        'notebook.NotebookApp.nbserver_extensions: 'my-serverextension = my_serverextension'
    }
)
```

Where, because `nbserver_extensions` is a dict of importable locations of the magic-named function.

Anyhoo, thinking about that, though: some of the more powerful/evil "extensions" out there aren't serverextensions (or aren't _just_ server extensions), and need to touch other parts of (multiple) `jupyter_[*_]config`.

Having a "one entry point to rule them all" for config would be pretty concise:

```python
setup(
    # ...
    entry_points={
        'jupyter_notebook_config': 'my-contents-kernel-auth-manager-serverextension = mckam:my_custom_jupyter_config'
    }
)
# ...
def my_custom_jupyter_config(c):
    c.NotebookApp.contents_manager_class = Foo
    c.NotebookApp.nbserverextensions["foo"] = Bar
    ...
```

Presumably this would take effect right before the on-disk `$PREFIX/etc/jupyter`, such that it could _still_ be disabled/whatever down the line by file configuration.

As an extension developer, you're still writing a function, but it's not a magic-named function, and the magic name appears in the least-worst place (`setup.py`), but you can configure whatever you need to affect the change.

The typical reasonig for shortcut is the following:

No shortcut should use Alt (or Shift but in a lesser measure) by default. None. Ever. 

Many non-english keyboard need Alt and or Shift to type characters. So Shift and Alt are not available.

Typically, french keyboard use Shift for Numbers. So `Cmd-Shift-[` is actually "`Cmd-5`" on a french keyboard... which is taken, and on French keyboard typing `[` actually require pressing `Alt-Shift-5`... so I'm not even sure how that will conflict resolve, or if this could trigger ghost shortcuts.

Hence I would prefer _not_ to do it. What we can (and maybe should) do, is provide a set of "nice but not enabled by default" keymap that are a click away, and provide a "Querty-layout" extra, "Azerty layout extra", "Cyrillic extra", where we can go as crazy as we like.

It turns out that in order for `flatten` and `expand` to work, `ListType` must accept dictionaries because there's no way for `expand` to disambiguate between a list and a dictionary. This may help explain why the coercion behavior of `ListType` is so lax.

A couple of options come to mind:

* Put loose behavior behind a `coerce=True` setting. Deal with `expand` by implementing a heuristic whereby a mapping whose keys match an uninterruptible sequence of natural numbers `[0, 1, ...]` is presumed to be a list.

* Preserve coercion rules for iterables but don't create a 1-element list to hold the value as a last resort, which is what this specific issue is about.

I think I'm going to go with the second option for the time being. If strict/loose options are going to be springing up all over the place, I'd like there to be a well-defined system for overriding them at import time as well.

> An additional convenience would be to provide a function which returns the gradient of a scalar field; this would be a vector field containing all the partial derivatives. This may be too specialized for inclusion in the library, though.

> Yes, I want to evaluate the gradient over an entire grid. 

I think this is exactly what you get with `grad`'s vectorizing behavior, provided you set up the inputs correctly. For example, consider the electric potential due to a pair of uniformly charged, nonconducting, hollow spheres. `grad` gives you the electric field in a single step.

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad

def sphere_potential(xy):
    R = 0.5
    r = np.sqrt(np.sum(xy**2, axis=-1))
    return np.minimum(1/r, 1/R)

def potential(xy):
    sphere_pos = np.array([0., 1.])
    return (  sphere_potential(xy - sphere_pos)
            - sphere_potential(xy + sphere_pos))

X, Y = np.meshgrid(*[np.linspace(-2, 2, 50)]*2)
xy_grid = np.stack([X, Y], axis=-1)

potential_grid = potential(xy_grid)
E_field_grid = -grad(potential)(xy_grid)  # <--- evaluating the electric field

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.figure()
plt.pcolormesh(X, Y, potential_grid)
plt.quiver(X, Y, E_field_grid[..., 0], E_field_grid[..., 1])
```

Define your module like this:

```
git module foo:
  url: https://github.com/foo/bar
  reup: my_branch
```

Here we're using the reup *field*, which tells the reup *command* what to do. This causes the `foo` module to look at `my_branch` to get the latest rev when you reup, instead of looking at the `master` branch.

I'm a little worried that the name `reup` is confusing when we use it for both the command and the field, even though they are related. We can't call the field `branch`, because that could get confused with the the `rev` field. (Note that the `reup` field does *not* affect what branch gets fetched by the `sync` command. That is only affected by the `rev` field, and it always defaults to `master` if the `rev` field is not set.) We could name it something more verbose, like `reup branch` or `reup source`. The problem with the add your own (of any alg, curve, byte form) approach is that that the keys will end up being...  any alg, any curve, any form. And as I understand, with no labels to mark what 'public_key' represents. This makes it tough to rely on those keys being found in a known location in a form that can be useful by applications built on this stack.

It's of course fine if you're not in the business of being a PKI, just as long as it is understood by those of us who may need to build something on top of that infrastructure that we will be responsible for the the creation, storage, retrieval, and consumption of those keys end-to-end. So end users would need to generate keys, manage the private key, and get the pub key into your zone file in a known location on their own.

Do you think that is a fair characterization?

There has been a lot of discussion on how to best model actions in reactive and there seems to be general agreement that actions are fundamentally not reactive, in that they are an explicit, user generated invocation rather than a reaction to an event which represents a change in the state of the system, such as hook.  I think that the desired integration with the reactive pattern boils down to three things:

* Ensuring that the actions use the same Python interpreter and library paths
* The ability to guard an action based on reactive states
* The ability for handlers to be triggered based on state changes made in the action

The first isn't relevant to this PR.  The second can be done using `is_state` instead of `@when` decorators and fits more with the imperative nature of actions.  And I think that the third is better addressed through thoughtful redesign of how the bus functions to enable state changes to come from non-hook sources in a more natural way, for which I've started a discussion on #90.  In the meantime, however, the final point can also be easily worked around with a one line call to invoke the reactive bus.

Therefore, I don't want to move forward with adding new primitives to the framework to support this, and I'm going to close this PR in favor of the discussion in #90.

There is nothing that specifically states that the import order is case sensitive in the docs, but I can confirm with empirical usage that it does sort capital letters before lowercase. References for available options below:

https://www.jetbrains.com/help/pycharm/code-style-python.html

# OPTIONS
### Sort import statements
Select or clear this checkbox to enable or disable sorting imports within individual import groups according to PEP 8.
### Sort imported names in "from" imports
If this checkbox is selected, the imports in the `from ... import ...` statements are sorted alphabetically
```python
# Selected
from sys import modules, path, version

# Not selected
from sys import version, path, modules
```
### Sort plain and "from" imports separately within a group
If this checkbox is not selected, the imports from the same module, regardless of their type, are grouped together, but so that import statements go first, and `from ... import ...` statements go next.
If this checkbox is selected, the imports are at first sorted by there type (first import, next `from ... import ...`), and then alphabetically.
```python
# Selected
import os
import sys
from os import getenv
from sys import path

# Not selected
import os
from os import getenv
import sys
from sys import path
```
The option Sort plain and 'from' imports separately within a group corresponds to the option force_alphabetical_sort of the utility isort.

Also, this sorting order corresponds to Google Python Style Guide

### Join "from" imports with the same source
If this checkbox is selected, the "from" imports of the same source are combined.
```python
# Selected
from os.path import join, relpath, abspath, dirname, basename

# Not selected
from os.path import join
from os.path import relpath, abspath
from os.path import dirname, basename
```
The option Join "from" imports with the same source corresponds to the combine_as option of the the utility isort

This post is an aside regarding API design. If you're just trying to get things done, you can safely skip reading this post.

A RESTful way to say "make content view version 5 belong to environments 2, 9 and 10" is this:

    PUT
    /katello/api/v2/content_view_versions/5
    {'environment': [2, 9, 10]}

A method call in the form above works when you want to explicitly list all of the environments that a content view version should belong to. However, it doesn't allow you express thoughts like "make content view version 5 belong to whatever environments it already does, plus environment 11." We can modify the call to make that happen:

    PUT
    /katello/api/v2/content_view_versions/5
    {'environment': {'add': [11]}}

We can even express even more complex statements, like "make content view version 5 belong to whatever environments it already does, minus environment 10, plus environment 11."

    PUT
    /katello/api/v2/content_view_versions/5
    {'environment': {'remove': [10], 'add': [11]}}

The developers chose to let clients express thoughts like that in a different way. As SatelliteQE/robottelo#2096 shows, this method call is possible:

    DELETE
    /katello/api/v2/content_view_versions/:id/environments/:environment_id

And that's what you're doing here. (Annoyingly, the path above is not listed in the API documentation.) The above can also *probably* be expressed as so:

    DELETE
    /katello/api/v2/content_view_versions/:id
    {'environment_id': …}

I think that the latter form is preferable, and it's what I've suggested, but either form should work.

Just breaking out of the loop should work, so I think this should be possible:

```python
iter_hooks = pm.ihook.myproject_get_password(arg1=1, arg2=2)
for result in iter_hooks:
    if not result:  # skip to the next hookimpl
        continue
    else:
        success = unlock_with_pw(result)
        if success:
            break
```

Or more succinctly:

```python
iter_hooks = pm.ihook.myproject_get_password(arg1=1, arg2=2)
for result in iter_hooks:
    success = unlock_with_pw(result)
    if success:
        break
``` 

And hook wrappers would still work, you can call pre/post if you use a try/finally while yielding the hook results. Here's a short demonstration:

```python


def call_hooks():
    r = []
    try:
        hool_impls = range(5)
        for x in hool_impls:
            yield x
            r.append(x)
    finally:
        print('call post hookwrappers', r)


for i in call_hooks():
    print('hook', i)
    if i == 2:
        print('breaking')
        break
```

This prints:

``` 
hook 0
hook 1
hook 2
breaking
call post hookwrappers [0, 1]
```

@Mudpuppy12 yes

`api.tm.auth.users.user.create(**params)`

can be used to create an account. For example, the following

```
name = "foo"
password = "secret"
api.tm.auth.users.user.create(name=foo, password=secret)
```

Modifying can be done by first loading the object, then changing parameters, and then calling `update()`

```
user = api.tm.auth.users.user.load(name="foo")
user.description = "bar"
user.update()
```

Deleting can be done by doing the aforementioned `load` and then calling `.delete()` on the object.

```
user = api.tm.auth.users.user.create(name="foo")
user.delete()
```

A quick way to disable the warning should be:
```
from requests.packages.urllib3.exceptions import InsecureRequestWarning
requests.packages.urllib3.disable_warnings(InsecureRequestWarning)
```

Hi All, I think @richlander is close to having something to post for .NET Core 2.1 all up. 

I know from a .NET Core Runtime perspective, the major focus for 2.1 has been performance. There are new API's like Span and Memory, many API's are also now leveraging those under the hood as well. We also have improvements in many other areas underway. We've significantly improved Networking performance and done a few different optimizations in the IO stack. Beyond that, we've be addressing feedback on features that shipped in 2.0 and doing general bug fixing.

As far as the community helping. I'd love to see how we can make that easier. At least in the corefx and coreclr repos, we've been marking things as up-for-grabs. We're also putting things in the 2.1 milestone that we would like to see get done in 2.1. Any help on work in that milestone from the community would be awesome to get. If there is a particular area or item of interest, I'd be happy to help identify good things to pick up and try to get someone from Microsoft to be on point to make sure support is available and questions can get answered.

For setups with an alias into a letsencrypt directory even removing the 'acme-challenge' directory would be the wrong thing to do, because that would leave the alias point to a non existing directory.

    Alias "/.well-known/acme-challenge" "/srv/letsencrypt/example.com/.well-known/acme-challenge"

On the other hand, adding a /srv/letsencrypt/example.com/.well-known/acme-challenge/.keep file of course allows to work around the deletion if the acme-challenge directory too.

This kind of alias structure is nice because things are cleanly separated and an concurrent update (like rsync -delete) in the normal location will not mess up certificate renewal.

I agree this PR should be merged before getting any larger. I'm not convinced that the hackery can't go in, but I'm not convinced it needs to either. I think that the changes necessary to remove it and temporarily remove support for SimpleHTTP should be fairly minimal and I see no reason to rush support for SimpleHTTP into Standalone. You could obviously continue working off any changes here that aren't merged into master.

I'm also slightly concerned about doing a such a large rewrite like this so close to launch. This introduces a lot of new code with the potential for a lot of new bugs. Standalone needs to be absolutely rock solid for launch and while the previous version had some problems in the past, I haven't seen any bug reports in a long time.

With the exception of my comments, I think this looks good. I tested it a number of times without issue. Thanks for all the hard work @kuba. Given the size of this PR, @jdkasten or @pde, would you like to take a look at this?

This is a tricky design question that we've looked at before but may have to revisit.

The challenge is that version dates are for describing backwards-incompatible changes, and setting a default version date in the SDK makes every version date change a breaking change for the SDK, so that update the SDK could break your code.

This is similar to the motivation for not having default version dates at the service API level: https://github.com/watson-developer-cloud/api-guidelines#why-make-the-version-date-required-wouldnt-it-be-simpler-to-make-it-optional. The SDK is potentially easier to address, but is still challenging, especially since it combines so many different separate services that are each independently versioned. The combination of multiple, separately versioned services is one difference of this SDK to other SDKs that wrap a single version-dated service.

For now the recommended (and enforced) use of the SDK is to provide a version date when constructing a service object. We make the sample code and document use the latest version, so users should copy and paste the code at authoring and update is as desired. In exchange, they shouldn't have to worry that upgrading the SDK might break their code, even when we introduce new version-dates.

I also think that the `--keepDays N` option should keep a history, so that any state of the past N days can be restored. Simply replacing the modtime with the upload time doesn't solve every problem though. Suppose you actually uploaded a file 1 year ago, delete it locally and then do a sync. I solve this trouble in my library (marshmallow intregration with mongoengine) by creating a mixin class which overload the `_serialize` method


```python
class SkipEmptyClass(object):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        skip_empty = kwargs.pop('skip_empty', True)
        super(SkipEmptyClass, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.skip_empty = skip_empty

    def _serialize(self, value, attr, obj):
        value = super(SkipEmptyClass, self)._serialize(value, attr, obj)
        if (self.skip_empty and
                (value is None or isinstance(value, (list, tuple)) and not value)):
            return missing
        return value

# Overload your marshmallow field class here
class String(SkipEmptyClass, fields.String):  # watch out inheritance order with MRO
    pass
```

SFrames are lazily evaluated, so SFrame operations which you're performing aren't actually being calculated until the data is required. The `sf.materialize()` method forces that SFrame to perform all of the calculations in its graph, so all previous operations done on this SFrame are finalized.
`materialize` is usually useful if you've performed several pre-processing steps, and are about to iterate over the same SFrame (as in the case of several training epochs etc.).
Nevertheless, materializing is irrelevant in your case, since SFrames built by `SFrameBuilder` will always be materialized.

However - using `SFrameBuilder` might explain the HD explosion, as the SframeBuilder uses a very large cache, as described in https://github.com/apple/turicreate/issues/102.

The behaviour of create_append() is a bit unfortunate and I'm not happy with it. If the file doesn't exist, it will be created, otherwise appended, and that's what we want. But, especially in interactive use, you often start creating a file, your code crashes, you fix the bug, then start creating the file again. Only now you will be appending to the previous half-baked file. I will think of a better way to do this.

There are three reasons I can think of that that would happen:

1.  `jt.joinFiles` is `True`
2.  You've specified `-j yes` in `jt.nativeSpecification`
3.  Your SGE cluster has `-j yes` set by default.  (This is a pretty common setting.)

I would suggest trying to manually set `jt.joinFiles` to `False` and see if that helps (and making sure that you're not setting `-j yes` in `jt.nativeSpecification`).

@wholmgren Cliff had concerns about documenting my changes to the published algorithm. Any suggestions here? I'd like to get a thorough review after posting a description somewhere good.

I'll admit I'm intrigued with the idea that we can wrap the calcparams and specify the model with a keyword.  I think the challenge will be to manage the model parameters. There's no getting around the fact that each model has some unique values, and that the parameter sets we have to offer are only for one of the diode models (the CEC model). 

I think the flexibility to mix and match auxiliary equations has value but not to most users.  There is value is our current API for most users.  Using separate functions for each model forces us to be explicit and transparent about the equations are used in each model - and these are primary objectives of pvlib.

So despite the intriguing nature of the wrapper, I'm hesitant to say let's replace the 'one calcparams per model' approach.

For the 'hybrid' model you propose, I'm ok with that as long as there is a published reference documenting the model being submitted.

I think there were two potential actions:

1) Require timestamped data to be localized. I don't know that we agreed
whether this was a good requirement or not. I view it as moderately
positive, because it will force all handling of timezones to be clearer.
But a potential drawback is when I somehow find myself using a Central
timezone for data in an Eastern location because of how the data was
captured originally. (Probably because it wasn't clear whether the data was
captured as interval beginning or interval ending, to use PVsyst's
nomenclature.)

2) Provide a mapping to true solar time. I believe this is a desirable
feature, but not likely to be part of the 0.2 release.

Finally, I do think that better documentation can cover item 1 if others
don't prefer requiring localization.

Z3_solver Z3_API Z3_solver_translate(Z3_context source, Z3_solver s, Z3_context target);

This would let you spawn a new context, and translate a solver object into the new context.

Z3's API already has Z3_ast_vector_translate, which lets you convert an ast-vector from one context to another. Z3_solver_get_assertions returns the current assertions in a solver as an ast_vector,

@wintersteiger It turned out this was a **very deep rabbit hole**.

For this particular example the developers tell me that it is okay for the generated assembly to read uninitialized memory because this is not observable from the original program. It doesn't matter what the value of the uninitialized `bool` is because the assembly code will eventually jump to right place meaning that from the perspective of the C++ program this non-deterministic jump is not visible.

The MemorySanitizer folks tell me that is actually a false positive from valgrind.

Accessor functions on matching constructors are defined as you would expect, so a head of a cons(x,y) returns x. But accessor functions on non-matching constructors, e.g., head(nil) are _underspecified_, in other words their meaning is not fixed by the theory. So it is possible to have head(nil) be 1 or 2 (but not both).

> I don't know if there is a specific reason that Lasagne is accessing the backend directly

Yes, the reason is that `conv2d` interface did not support all possible options at the time, so we had to have extra layer types to be able to do `same` convolutions, for example.

> Lasagne should not explicitly use `theano.sandbox.cuda` in the code

I'd prefer to have the implementation-specific layers still use the specific implementation, there might be special use cases where you want a specific convolution not to be handled by cuDNN -- this is not easily possible otherwise.

> If so, how can I test that it is using Cpu or the old backend or the new backend?

That's exactly the question you'd need to find the answer to. We need a way to replace the initial checking at `lasagne.layers.corrmm` (https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/corrmm.py) with something like:
```python
if theano currently uses a gpu with gpuarray:
    from theano.gpuarray import GpuCorrMM
elif theano currently uses a gpu with sandbox.cuda:
    from theano.sandbox.cuda import GpuCorrMM
else:
    raise ImportError explaining that theano needs to be configured to use a gpu
```
It's not enough to merely check `theano.config.device`, because there are other ways to enable the GPU (we've been there before: #606). And we should not import `theano.sandbox.cuda` unless we already know that it's needed, so we need to place the gpuarray check first.

For `layers.dnn`, we additionally need to check whether DNN is available, but this should be easy once we know if and which GPU backend is in use.

For `layers.cuda_convnet`, we need to error out if the new backend is used -- this only works with the old backend.

When this is done, we will also need to update the hacks needed to build the Sphinx docs for the GPU. For more information, see:
https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/823#issuecomment-291807733
https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/823#issuecomment-291816840

It would also be helpful if you could just find out what's the equivalence of `theano.sandbox.cuda.cuda_enabled` in the new backend (i.e., a way to tell if the new backend is in use, regardless of whether it was enabled via the configuration flag or some other means). I can then probably do the rest relatively quickly.
